specification.topic-statement: The UUID generation system produces a 22-character base64url-encoded identifier from 128 random bits with UUID v4 version and variant fields applied.

scope.in-scope: Random byte production, UUID v4 version and variant bit application, base64url encoding, and memory ownership of the returned identifier.
scope.boundary: This system draws random bytes from the platform's non-cryptographic sequential RNG. The RNG is seeded once at application startup — this system does not seed the RNG; it only consumes values from it.

This system accepts a parent memory context and returns an identifier string allocated within that context. The caller is responsible for managing the lifetime of the parent context; when the parent context is released, the identifier is released with it.

behavior.generating-an-identifier:
The generation of an identifier proceeds in three steps in fixed order: random byte collection, UUID field application, and base64url encoding.
Sixteen bytes are collected by calling the platform's non-cryptographic sequential RNG sixteen times, taking the low byte of each call's result.
The RNG used is not cryptographically secure. The identifiers produced by this system are not suitable for purposes requiring cryptographic uniqueness or unpredictability.
The RNG is seeded at application startup by XORing the current time in seconds with the process ID. Identifiers generated by two instances of the application started within the same second and assigned the same process ID will follow identical byte sequences and produce identical identifiers.
Two of the sixteen bytes are modified before encoding:
The ninth byte (index 6, zero-based) has its upper four bits replaced with the value 4, establishing the UUID version field. Its lower four bits retain their random values.
The ninth byte from the start (index 8, zero-based) has its upper two bits set to binary 10, establishing the RFC 4122 variant field. Its lower six bits retain their random values.
All other bytes are left unmodified.
After field application, the effective randomness in the identifier is 122 bits: 128 bits minus the 4 fixed version bits and 2 fixed variant bits.
The sixteen bytes are encoded using the base64url alphabet defined in RFC 4648 §5: the uppercase letters A through Z, the lowercase letters a through z, the digits 0 through 9, the hyphen character, and the underscore character. No padding characters are appended.
The bytes are grouped in triplets and each triplet produces four encoded characters. The final group contains only one byte, which produces two encoded characters. The total output is 22 characters.
The result is a continuous 22-character string with no separators, delimiters, or hyphens in UUID-conventional positions.
The output is not the conventional hyphenated hexadecimal UUID format (8-4-4-4-12 hex digits). It is a compact base64url string. Hyphens may appear in the output as encoding characters, not as separators, at positions determined by the random content.
behavior.memory-allocation-failure: If allocation of the output string fails, the application terminates immediately. No error value is returned to the caller.
